food_subgroup: Pizza — thin crust, vegetable and cheese topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza food product class; no specific entry for thin-crust pizza with grilled vegetables and buffalo mozzarella DOP. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pizza as a category. No specific facet code for thin-crust vegetable-and-bufala variant; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226695000 is the generic pizza concept; no specific code for this variant. Closest parent used.

main_ingredients: organic grilled mixed vegetables (zucchini, eggplant, bell peppers, or similar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole 12-inch thin crust pizza (approximately 400 g), as typically served at an Italian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Pizza, cheese, thin crust — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods data for grilled vegetables and buffalo mozzarella component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry matches this exact product (thin crust pizza with organic grilled mixed vegetables, tomato sauce, buffalo mozzarella DOP, and EVO oil). Values are estimated by weighted component assembly: thin pizza crust dough (~40% by weight, USDA SR Legacy 21299 pizza crust), tomato sauce (~15%, USDA SR Legacy 11549), buffalo mozzarella (~25%, USDA FDC 1098061 mozzarella whole milk), grilled mixed vegetables (~15%, USDA SR Legacy zucchini/eggplant/peppers average), and extra virgin olive oil (~5%, USDA SR Legacy 04053). Per-100g values are derived from this weighted blend. Energy cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entry 21299 (cheese pizza, thin crust) as a sanity anchor.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for thin-crust pizza
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for pizza in the range of 36–60 depending on crust thickness and toppings. Thin-crust cheese pizza is reported at approximately 50 (GI Database food ID 1664, pizza, Super Supreme, thin crust, Pizza Hut; and related thin-crust entries). A GI of 50 is used here as a defensible analogous estimate for this thin-crust vegetable-and-bufala pizza. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (50 × 106) / 100 = 53.0, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 26.5 × 4 = 106 g. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per whole pizza serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two slices, as a full 12-inch pizza provides approximately 1680 mg sodium — approaching or exceeding the 1500–2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load per serving (GL ~53 for whole pizza) despite low GI (50) due to large carbohydrate portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a controlled portion (one to two slices, approximately 100–150 g) to limit glycemic load; the thin crust and vegetable toppings reduce GI compared to thick-crust or meat-heavy pizzas, but total carbohydrate intake must still be monitored within the individual's daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from buffalo mozzarella (~3.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this pizza is preferable to processed-meat pizzas due to its vegetable content and use of extra virgin olive oil (a source of cardioprotective monounsaturated fats), but saturated fat from buffalo mozzarella and sodium from the crust and sauce warrant portion control to stay within AHA guidelines of less than 13 g saturated fat and less than 1500 mg sodium per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Verdure e Bufala Pizza is a 12-inch Italian thin-crust pizza featuring organic grilled mixed vegetables (typically zucchini, eggplant, and bell peppers), a tomato sauce base, authentic buffalo mozzarella DOP (Denominazione di Origine Protetta), and organic extra virgin olive oil. The thin crust is baked until crisp, providing a light, slightly charred base that contrasts with the soft, creamy buffalo mozzarella and the smoky, tender grilled vegetables. Buffalo mozzarella DOP is a protected-designation cheese made from the milk of water buffalo, prized for its delicate milky flavour, high moisture content, and superior melt quality compared to standard cow's milk mozzarella. The use of organic extra virgin olive oil adds fruity, peppery notes and contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids. Nutritionally, this pizza provides approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 9.2 g, 8.5 g fat (of which 3.8 g is saturated), and 26.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low GI), though the glycemic load for a full pizza serving is substantial (~53) due to the large carbohydrate portion. Sodium is a clinical concern at approximately 420 mg per 100 g. The dish is vegetarian and contains gluten and dairy allergens. It is served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk in Singapore and is representative of contemporary Italian restaurant pizza in the Southeast Asian market.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exactly matches this restaurant-specific product. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for thin-crust pizza dough, tomato sauce, whole-milk mozzarella, grilled vegetables, and extra virgin olive oil. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for thin-crust pizza. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported and the assembly methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no single entry for this exact product exists in any approved primary source. 2. Buffalo mozzarella DOP has higher moisture and lower fat than standard mozzarella; values adjusted accordingly using USDA FDC 1098061 (mozzarella, whole milk) as the closest available proxy. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this food type in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used. 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for thin-crust pizza; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 5. Serving size of 400 g represents a whole 12-inch thin-crust pizza; individual slice serving (~100 g) would yield GL ~13.3. 6. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names are required or provided. 7. The food_group 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record as pizza is grain-based. 8. Allergens: gluten (wheat crust) and dairy (buffalo mozzarella). 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component assembly and are approximate. 10. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation.
